segments.p2-3-g1: This whole clause is simple past: Я (I) is the subject and застал is the perfective past of застать, meaning 'came upon/found (unexpectedly)'. У него is a prepositional phrase (у + genitive) meaning 'at his (place)' and indicates location/possession; ещё двух моих школьных товарищей is the direct object expressed with the numeral + noun. Because the direct object is animate masculine plural, the numeral and noun appear in the genitive form (двух, товарищей) though syntactically it's the object of застал. Ещё here means 'another/two more.'
segments.p2-3-g2: Они is the subject 'they'; толковали is the past imperfective plural of толковать and here means 'were discussing/talking over' or 'interpreting/explaining' — a past continuous/habitual sense rather than a single completed action.
segments.p2-3-g3: По-видимому is an idiomatic parenthetical adverb meaning 'apparently' or 'it would seem'; formally it derives from по + dative видимому but functions as a sentence adverb expressing the speaker's inference or uncertainty.
segments.p2-3-g4: Об introduces the prepositional phrase 'об одном важном деле' = 'about one important matter.' Об requires the prepositional case (деле); одном and важном are prepositional singular modifiers agreeing with дело. The word order is normal for a prepositional object of разговор/толковать.
segments.p2-3-g5: На приход мой is an inverted (somewhat literary) order meaning 'on my arrival' (more neutral would be на мой приход). Ни один из них 'not one of them' uses ни to reinforce negation. Не обратил внимания is a past-tense construction meaning 'did not pay attention' — обратить внимание = 'to direct attention', and внимание is in the genitive (никакого внимания) because of the negation and quantity words.
segments.p2-3-g6: Что is the conjunction 'which/that' linking to the previous clause; было is the past-tense copula 'was'; даже is an adverbial intensifier 'even'; странно is a neuter predicate adjective/adverb meaning 'strange' — together: 'which was even strange.'
segments.p2-3-g7: Потому что introduces the reason 'because.' Я не видался с ними is the past reflexive verb видеться in negative: 'I hadn't met/seen them' (видаться с кем = to meet/see someone). Уж годы is an idiomatic time expression meaning 'for years already' (уж = already/indeed, годы = years).
segments.p2-3-g8: Очевидно is a sentence adverb (fronted and followed by a comma) that signals the speaker's inference or conclusion; here it functions like English 'apparently' or 'obviously.'
segments.p2-3-g9: меня is the accusative pronoun 'me' as the direct object of считали (past tense, plural — 'they considered'). считали is the past tense plural of считать meaning 'to consider/regard.' чем-то вроде is a fixed phrase meaning 'something like / sort of,' and what follows — самой обыкновенной мухи — is in the genitive (вроде governs the genitive), with самой (intensifying 'the very/just') and обыкновенной agreeing in feminine genitive singular with мухи (genitive singular of муха = 'fly').
segments.p2-3-g31: Мосье Зверков is the nominative subject (a French title used as part of the name). Был is the past-tense copula 'was'. Все время is an adverbial phrase meaning 'all the while' or 'constantly'. И here functions as 'also / as well', linking this role to others already implied. Моим школьным товарищем is a predicate in the instrumental case after быть to state someone's relation or role: '(my) schoolmate.'
segments.p2-3-g32: Я is the subject 'I'. Особенно is an adverb 'especially / particularly'. Стал + infinitive (стал ненавидеть) is a periphrastic past construction meaning 'began to [do]' (imperfective, indicating the start of a developing state). Его is the accusative object 'him'. С + genitive (с высших классов) marks the starting point in time — 'from the higher classes (onward).'
segments.p2-3-g33: В низших классах is a temporal/adverbial phrase (в + locative/plural) meaning 'in the lower classes' or 'in the lower grades'. Он был is the past-tense copula 'he was'. Только qualifies the predicate as 'only/merely'. Хорошенький is a diminutive adjective ('pretty' / 'nice') agreeing with он in gender and number.
segments.p2-3-g34: Резвый is an adjective 'lively / frisky' modifying мальчик. Мальчик is the noun 'boy' in the nominative, completing the predicate started by был.
segments.p2-3-g35: Которого is the relative pronoun in the accusative masculine singular referring back to мальчик and functioning as the object of любили. Все is the subject 'everyone'. Любили is the past plural of любить agreeing with все; here it means 'loved' / 'were fond of'.
segments.p2-3-g36: Я is the subject pronoun 'I'; the comma shows a slight pause/contrast before the adverb that follows.
segments.p2-3-g37: Впрочем is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'however' or 'nevertheless', used to introduce a contrast or qualification.
segments.p2-3-g38: Ненавидел is the past-tense verb 'hated' (masculine singular). Его is the accusative object 'him'. И in this position adds emphasis meaning 'even' (i.e. 'even in the lower classes'). В низших классах, as before, is the temporal phrase 'in the lower classes'.
segments.p2-3-g39: И is a coordinating conjunction 'and'. Именно is an intensifier 'exactly / precisely'. За то (за + то) here means 'for that' or 'because of that' — the phrase introduces the reason that follows.
segments.p2-3-g40: Что introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that / because'. Он был is 'he was'. Хорошенький и резвый мальчик is the predicate nominal with two coordinated adjectives ('pretty and lively') plus the noun 'boy' in the nominative, giving the reason for the hatred.
segments.p2-3-g10: 'Так' is an adverb here meaning 'in that way/like that' placed at the start for emphasis. The verb 'третировали' is past tense, plural, imperfective (they treated/used to treat) and is negated by 'не' before it; the subject is omitted (implied 'they/people'). 'Меня' is the accusative object. 'Даже в школе' is an adverbial phrase ('even at school') placed after the verb to emphasize that not even at school was I treated that way.
segments.p2-3-g11: 'Хотя' introduces a concessive clause ('although'). 'Все' here is a pronoun meaning 'everyone' (the people in question). 'Там' is the locative adverb 'there' and 'ненавидели' is past tense, plural, imperfective: 'hated' (a general/habitual past attitude). Word order is subject ('все') — object ('меня') — adverb ('там') — verb ('ненавидели'), a normal Russian ordering for emphasis on 'everyone'.
segments.p2-3-g12: 'Я' is the nominative personal pronoun 'I' and here simply marks the speaker as the subject; the comma signals a break before the parenthetical 'конечно'.
segments.p2-3-g13: 'Конечно' is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'of course' or 'certainly' and modifies the whole clause that follows; it's separated by commas as a discourse marker.
segments.p2-3-g14: 'Понимал' is past tense, masculine, singular, imperfective: 'I understood' or 'I realized' (a continuing/mental state rather than a punctual perfective). The comma after it leads into the subordinate clause introduced by 'что'.
segments.p2-3-g15: 'Что' begins the subordinate clause ('that'). The verbal cluster 'должны были презирать' is a past-modal construction: 'должны' (modal 'ought to/should') plus the past auxiliary 'были' produces the sense 'ought to have/should have despised' with the infinitive 'презирать'. The subject 'они' is 'they'. 'Меня' is the accusative object. 'Теперь' is the adverb 'now' placed before the reason phrase introduced by 'за' ('for'). 'За' here means 'for' (indicating cause), and it governs the noun phrases that follow ('за неуспех моей служебной карьеры' — 'for the failure of my official career') and then 'и за то,' which introduces the next explanatory clause.
segments.p2-3-g16: This is a subordinate clause introduced again by 'что' ('that / the fact that') and completes 'за то' ('for the fact that...'). 'Я' is the subject; 'уж очень' is a colloquial intensifier meaning 'really/very' ('уж' here strengthens 'очень'). 'Опустился' is a past reflexive perfective (or resultative past) meaning 'sank down / fell into a low state / degraded' — it denotes that the speaker had fallen to a lower social/ moral condition.
segments.p2-3-g17: 'Ходил' is past imperfective (here habitual/past-tense) and with clothing phrases often means 'went about / used to go' wearing something; it expresses repeated or characteristic action in the past. 'В дурном платье' is a prepositional phrase 'in shabby/bad clothes' where 'дурном' describes poor quality clothing. 'И проч.' is an abbreviation of 'и прочее' meaning 'and so on / etc.' used to truncate a list.
segments.p2-3-g18: This segment is just the comma/punctuation connecting the previous clause to the explanatory clause that follows; it marks a pause and clause boundary.
segments.p2-3-g19: 'Что' again introduces the explanatory clause ('that / which'). 'В их глазах' is a fixed locative expression meaning 'in their eyes' or 'in their view' — 'их' is the possessive/oblique form referring to 'them' and 'глазах' is the locative/plural form meaning 'in the sight/view of'. 'Составляло' is past tense, neuter singular (agreeing with the abstract 'что/то') of 'составлять' meaning 'constituted' or 'amounted to'. 'Вывеску' is the accusative object ('a sign/placard'); the following genitive phrase 'моей неспособности и мелкого значения' names what that sign advertised: 'of my inability and petty/insignificant importance' (i.e., my inability and small significance).
segments.p2-3-g20: 'Но' is the adversative conjunction 'but'. 'Я' is the subject. 'Все-таки' (split here as 'все' + 'таки') is a colloquial adverbial particle meaning 'after all / nevertheless / still' that softens or stresses the speaker's reservation. 'Не ожидал' is past tense, masculine singular, 'did not expect'. 'До такой степени' is a standard expression 'to such a degree/extent' where 'до' governs the genitive 'такой степени'. 'Презрения' is a genitive noun here governed by 'до' (literally 'to such a degree of contempt'), so the whole phrase means 'I did not expect contempt to such an extent.'
segments.p2-3-g21: This is a simple past-tense clause: the proper name comes first (subject), then the adverb 'даже' as an emphatic modifier, and the verb 'удивился' is the past singular perfective of 'удивиться' meaning a single completed reaction ('was surprised'). The object 'моему приходу' is in the dative because 'удивиться' governs the dative: literally 'was surprised to/at my arrival.'
segments.p2-3-g22: 'Он' is the subject 'he.' The phrase 'и прежде' here functions adverbially as 'even before' or 'previously as well' (the particle 'и' adds emphasis/‘also’). 'Всегда' places the state as habitual or continual ('always'). 'Как будто' is a two-word comparative conjunction 'as if / as though' introducing the manner of the following verb. 'Удивлялся' is the imperfective past of 'удивляться', indicating an ongoing or habitual attitude ('used to be/sounded surprised'), and it also governs the dative 'моему приходу' ('at my coming').
segments.p2-3-g23: 'Все это' is the neuter nominal phrase 'all this' (subject), and 'озадачило' is the past neuter singular of the perfective verb 'озадачить' meaning 'puzzled' or 'perplexed.' 'Меня' is the accusative pronoun 'me' marking the person affected by that action: 'All this puzzled me.'
segments.p2-3-g24: 'Я сел' is a perfective past ('I sat down'). 'В некоторой тоске' uses 'в' + prepositional ('некоторой тоске') to mean 'in a certain melancholy' (некоторой = 'a certain/some' modifying тоске). 'И начал слушать' links with 'и' = 'and' plus 'начал' (past of 'начать', 'began') + infinitive 'слушать' to mean 'and began to listen.'
segments.p2-3-g25: 'О чем' ('about what') is the relative/interrogative phrase introducing the content of the listening; here 'чем' is the form of 'что' used after the preposition 'о.' 'Они толковали' is past plural of 'толковать' meaning 'they were discussing/expounding' — the imperfective past 'толковали' indicates an ongoing or habitual talk. The whole clause is the object of 'слушать': 'to listen to what they were discussing.'
segments.p2-3-g26: 'Шла речь' is an idiomatic impersonal/passive-like construction: the verb 'шла' (past of 'идти') with 'речь' means 'there was talk' or 'the subject under discussion was' — here 'шла' carries the sense 'proceeded/was going on.' The adjectives 'серьезная и даже горячая' follow 'речь' as predicate adjectives ('serious and even heated/passionate'), and 'о прощальном обеде' (preposition + prepositional) specifies the topic: 'about a farewell dinner.'
segments.p2-3-g27: 'Который' is a relative pronoun referring back to 'обеде' ('which' = 'the dinner which'). 'Хотели устроить' is past plural 'they wanted to arrange/hold' + infinitive 'устроить' (to arrange). Word order places the verb before the subject (эти господа) which is common in relative clauses: '[a dinner] which these gentlemen wanted to arrange.' 'Завтра же' adds immediacy: 'tomorrow itself/very next day' (the particle 'же' intensifies 'завтра').
segments.p2-3-g28: 'Сообща' is an adverb meaning 'together' or 'collectively' and modifies the verb phrase 'устроить' — 'to arrange together.' It stands alone as a concise adverbial.
segments.p2-3-g29: This is a dative participial phrase describing the recipient of the dinner: 'отъезжавшему ... их товарищу Зверкову' — 'to their comrade Zverkov, who was leaving.' 'Отъезжавшему' is the past active participle (masculine dative) of 'отъезжать' ('was leaving/was departing'), agreeing in case with 'товарищу' (dative = 'to the comrade'). 'Далеко в губернию' gives direction/distance ('far away to the province'). 'Их' is the possessive 'their' modifying 'товарищу', and 'Зверкову' is the comrade's name in dative.
segments.p2-3-g30: 'Служившему' is another past active participle in dative ('who served/was serving'), agreeing with 'товарищу' and specifying his occupation. 'Офицером' is in the instrumental case, the standard case to indicate role or office: 'serving as an officer.' Together: 'their comrade Zverkov, who was leaving far to the province, who served as an officer.'
segments.p2-3-g41: Учился он всегда постоянно плохо и чем дальше, — 'Учился' is past imperfective indicating ongoing study (he was a student); the personal pronoun 'он' follows the verb for slight emphasis. 'всегда постоянно плохо' stacks two adverbs (всегда, постоянно) for reinforcement, then the predicate adverb плохо describes the quality of his studying. The coordinating 'и' begins the correlative comparative clause 'чем дальше…'.
segments.p2-3-g42: тем хуже; — this is the second half of the correlative comparative 'чем... тем...', where 'тем' is the correlative particle ('the more/so much the') and 'хуже' is the comparative adjective meaning 'worse'. Together they mean 'the farther (on) the worse'.
segments.p2-3-g43: однако ж вышел из школы удачно, — 'однако' + enclitic 'ж' is a concessive particle pair meaning 'nevertheless/however'; 'вышел из школы' literally 'came out of the school' is a common way to say 'left/finished school' (past tense), and 'удачно' is an adverb qualifying how he left (successfully).
segments.p2-3-g44: потому что имел покровительство. — 'потому что' is the subordinating conjunction 'because'; 'имел' is the past imperfective 'had' (possession), and 'покровительство' is the noun 'patronage/protection' giving the reason for his successful exit from school.
segments.p2-3-g45: В последний год его в нашей школе ему досталось наследство, — 'В последний год' means 'in (his) last year' (time expression). The pronoun 'его' here is possessive 'his' modifying год; 'в нашей школе' is the locative phrase 'at our school'. 'ему досталось' is an impersonal/experiential construction meaning 'fell to him / he received' and takes the dative 'ему' plus the noun 'наследство' (inheritance).
segments.p2-3-g46: двести душ, — a nominal phrase giving the amount of the inheritance; 'душ' is the genitive plural of 'душа' used here in the old sense 'souls/people' (i.e., serfs or persons).
segments.p2-3-g47: а так как у нас все почти были бедные, — 'а так как' introduces a causal clause 'and since/as'; 'у нас' is the colloquial locative meaning 'among us' or 'here (in our circle)'; 'все почти были бедные' = 'almost everyone was poor' (были is past plural of 'to be').
segments.p2-3-g48: то он даже перед нами стал фанфаронить. — 'то' here is the result/so particle linking the clause ('so/then'); 'он' is the subject; 'даже' adds emphasis 'even'; 'перед нами' = 'in front of us / before us' (перед + instrumental); 'стал фанфаронить' is a two-word periphrasis where стал + infinitive/verb-in-imperfective sense indicates beginning to do something — 'began to boast' or 'took to boasting'.
segments.p2-3-g49: Это был пошляк в высшей степени, — 'Это был' is the simple past identification 'this/ he was'; 'пошляк' is a colloquial/slang noun meaning a vulgar or petty fellow; 'в высшей степени' is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'to the highest degree' (в + genitive 'степени' with the adjective 'высшей').
segments.p2-3-g50: но, — simple adversative conjunction 'but' introducing contrast with the following qualifier.
segments.p2-3-g51: однако ж, — another concessive pair 'however / nevertheless' (однако with the enclitic ж for emphasis), reinforcing the contrast begun by 'но'.
segments.p2-3-g52: добрый малый, — nominative noun phrase 'добрый малый' = 'a kind (добрый) lad/young fellow' (малый here = young fellow). The comma sets off the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.p2-3-g53: даже и тогда, — 'даже' ('even') plus intensifier 'и' with temporal adverb 'тогда' ('then'/'at that time'), preparing the subordinate temporal clause 'when he boasted'.
segments.p2-3-g54: когда фанфаронил. — subordinate temporal clause introduced by 'когда' ('when'); 'фанфаронил' is past imperfective meaning 'was boasting' or 'used to boast', describing the time when he nevertheless remained kind.
segments.p2-3-g55: У нас же, — 'У нас' is a locative colloquial expression meaning 'among us / in our circle'; the particle 'же' adds emphasis or contrast (roughly 'as for us' / 'yet among us').
segments.p2-3-g56: несмотря на наружные, — 'несмотря на' is the prepositional phrase 'despite' or 'in spite of'; 'наружные' is the adjective 'outward/external' qualifying the noun phrase that follows.
segments.p2-3-g57: фантастические и фразерские формы чести и гонора, — a list of modifiers and nouns: 'фантастические' ('fantastic, exaggerated') and 'фразерские' ('affected, phrase-mongering') modify 'формы' ('forms'); 'чести и гонора' ('honour and pride/arrogance') are the abstract notions whose external/formal manifestations are being criticised.
segments.p2-3-g58: все, — subject pronoun 'everyone' (collective) referring to the group being spoken of.
segments.p2-3-g59: кроме очень немногих, — 'кроме' ('except') plus the qualified phrase 'очень немногих' ('very few') excluding a small minority from the generalisation.
segments.p2-3-g60: даже увивались перед Зверковым, — 'даже' ('even') emphasises the verb 'увивались' (here reflexive/imperfective meaning 'fawned over' or 'flattered'); 'перед Зверковым' is 'in the presence of / before Zverkov' (instrumental), naming the person they fawned before.
segments.p2-3-g61: чем более он фанфаронил. — another correlative comparative: 'чем более... ' = 'the more...'; here 'чем' introduces the comparative clause 'the more he boasted', with 'более' as 'more' and 'он фанфаронил' as the clause giving the measure that increases the fawning.
segments.p2-3-g109: Subject + temporal/quantifying adverbs come before the verb as is normal in Russian: Он (he) + потом (afterward) + ещё несколько раз (a few more times). Оделевал is the imperfective past tense, indicating repeated or habitual past action (‘kept getting the better of’ / ‘overcame’). Меня is the direct object in accusative and follows the verb.
segments.p2-3-g110: Но is a simple adversative conjunction 'but'. Без governs the genitive (злобы) and here forms the phrase 'without malice' (absence of ill will).
segments.p2-3-g111: А introduces a mild contrast/shift. Как-то так is an idiomatic, colloquial cluster meaning 'in some such way / kind of like that' — как-то ('somehow/in a sort of way') + так ('so'). The particles together give a vague, offhand manner.
segments.p2-3-g112: Шутя is a деепричастие (adverbial participle) from шутить and describes the manner of the action — 'jokingly' or 'while joking'. It indicates an action simultaneous with the main verb.
segments.p2-3-g113: Мимоходом is an adverb meaning 'in passing' or 'incidentally'; like шутя, it modifies the manner/tone of the (overcoming) action and stresses its casual, fleeting character.
segments.p2-3-g114: Смеясь is another adverbial participle (from смеяться), 'laughing', and denotes the speaker of the verb acting while laughing — again showing manner and tone.
segments.p2-3-g115: Я is the subject. The adverbs злобно и презрительно precede the negated past verb and characterize the manner (how he would have answered). Не отвечал is the past imperfective negative 'did not answer' (imperfective stresses the action in the past, here simply negated), and ему is the dative 'to him'. The phrase means the speaker withheld an answer expressed in a malicious, contemptuous way.
segments.p2-3-g116: По выпуске is a temporal prepositional phrase 'on/after the graduation' (по + noun in prepositional). Он было сделал contains the particle было before the past verb — here it gives a tentative or momentary shade (an attempt or brief action) — and сделал ко мне шаг is literal 'made a step toward me' (i.e. 'made a move toward me').
segments.p2-3-g117: Я не очень противился — 'I didn't very much resist/oppose' (не очень = 'not very' softens the negation). Противился is past imperfective (I opposed/resisted), negated here to mean mild acquiescence.
segments.p2-3-g118: Потому что introduces the reason clause 'because'. Мне is dative ('to me'), это is the neuter demonstrative 'this/it' referring to his move, and польстило is past neuter of польстить used impersonally — 'it flattered me' / 'it was flattering to me'.
segments.p2-3-g119: Но introduces contrast 'but'. Мы скоро и естественно разошлись — 'we soon, and naturally, parted.' Разошлись is past plural reflexive verb 'to part company' or 'go different ways.' И естественно is an adverbial combination adding that the separation was natural/expected.
segments.p2-3-g120: Потом я слыхал — 'Later I heard' (слыхал is past imperfective 'have heard' or 'heard about'). Об его governs the genitive and introduces the topic 'about his ...'. Казарменно-поручичьих is a hyphenated, sardonic compound of adjectival forms modifying успехах and is in the appropriate case/number to agree with успехах (genitive/locative plural), literally 'barracks- and lieutenant-ish' successes; успехах is the plural locative/oblique of успех, 'successes' or 'achievements'.
segments.p2-3-g121: О том is the prenominal phrase 'about that' used to introduce a subordinate clause — literally 'about the matter that...'.
segments.p2-3-g122: Как introduces the content clause 'how he ...'. Он is the subject and кутит is present-tense imperfective colloquial verb 'to carouse/party' — here indicating habitual or ongoing behavior ('how he parties/carouses').
segments.p2-3-g123: Потом (temporal adverb) begins the clause meaning 'then/later'; пошли is past plural of пойти used here idiomatically to mean 'began to go/appear' (i.e. 'began to circulate'); другие modifies слухи ('other rumours'). The phrase 'о том' introduces the content of those rumours ('about the fact that...').
segments.p2-3-g124: как introduces an indirect question/indirect speech ('how...'); он is the grammatical subject; успевает is present tense imperfective meaning here 'makes progress / gets on / keeps up' in the career sense; по службе is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in (his) service / in matters of his position/career.'
segments.p2-3-g125: На улице is a locative time/place fronted phrase ('in/on the street'); the order Видел я/На улице он shows a common Russian inversion (locative + subject + verb). мне is the dative object 'to me'; уже не + past verb (кланялся) expresses 'no longer' (уже taking the 'no longer' sense when combined with negation). кланялся is past imperfective reflexive indicating habitual/past repeated bowing.
segments.p2-3-g126: и simply links clauses ('and'); я is the subject; подозревал is past imperfective 'I suspected'—it indicates an ongoing mental state in the past rather than a single concluded thought.
segments.p2-3-g127: что introduces the content clause ('that'); он is subject; боится is present-tense 'is afraid' and takes an infinitive to express 'afraid to do X'; компрометировать is the infinitive 'to compromise (his reputation)'; себя is the reflexive object referring back to the subject ('himself').
segments.p2-3-g128: раскланиваясь is a present active adverbial participle (деепричастие) meaning 'bowing repeatedly/doing a lot of bowing' and expresses the action by which he might compromise himself; с takes an instrumental to mark the person he bows to ('to/with'); такой незначительной is an attributive phrase in the instrumental (такой — demonstrative agreeing in case and gender) meaning 'such an insignificant (woman/person)'.
segments.p2-3-g129: как here is comparative 'as'/'like' introducing the example 'as I (am)'; я is nominative 'I' used in the simile construction 'such ... as I'.
segments.p2-3-g130: личностью is the noun in instrumental singular completing the prepositional structure with с ('with/ to such an insignificant person'); it names the role/quality of the speaker — 'an insignificant person.'
segments.p2-3-g131: Видел is past tense 'I saw' (here placed before the subject for stylistic emphasis: 'Видел я его' = 'I saw him'); я is the subject; его is the direct object 'him'; тоже 'also/too' links this observation to prior remarks; один раз 'once' is an adverbial time phrase; в театре gives the location 'at the theatre.'
segments.p2-3-g132: в третьем ярусе is a locative phrase with в + prepositional indicating the specific seat/level ('in the third tier'); третий is in the prepositional case (третьем) to agree with ярусе.
segments.p2-3-g133: уже here means 'already' (by that time); в + noun (аксельбантах in the prepositional plural) is a clothing phrase meaning 'in/ wearing shoulder belts' — a way of saying he was dressed in that element.
segments.p2-3-g134: Он is the subject 'he'; увивался is an imperfective reflexive verb here meaning 'fawned around' or 'twined himself about' (figurative for flattering/attentive behaviour); и links two verbs; изгибался is another reflexive imperfective verb 'bent/writhed/contorted himself' describing coquettish or exaggerated physical behaviour; перед takes the instrumental to mark the people addressed/present; дочками is instrumental plural 'the daughters'; одного древнего генерала is a genitive phrase specifying which daughters ('of an old/time‑honoured general'). The whole portrays repeated, theatrical deference.
segments.p2-3-g208: "сказал я в некотором волнении," is a past-tense reporting clause: сказа́л (past masculine) + subject я follows the verb (common Russian order for reported speech). в некотором волнении is an instrumental/locative phrase (в + prepositional) meaning "in a certain agitation," modifying the manner of speaking.
segments.p2-3-g209: даже here is an adverbial particle meaning "even" or an emphatic intensifier placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.p2-3-g210: по-видимому is a fixed adverb meaning "apparently" or "it would seem"; grammatically it's a compound where по functions as the particle and видимому is the short/dative-form element producing the modal sense.
segments.p2-3-g211: обидевшись is a perfective gerund (деепричастие) meaning "having taken offense" or "being offended," giving the reason/manner for the speaker's tone; it attaches to the main clause and is not an independent finite verb.
segments.p2-3-g212: "- если считать со мной," is a conditional clause introduced by если (if). считать со мной uses считать in the sense "to count" (i.e., include in a count) and со мной is the instrumental form meaning "with me" (i.e., "if you count me in").
segments.p2-3-g213: так будет не двадцать один, is a result/so-clause: так (in that case/then) + будет (future of быть, "will be") + negation не before the numeral (negating the amount). Word order places the negation before the numeral phrase.
segments.p2-3-g214: а двадцать восемь рублей. а is a coordinating particle introducing a contrast/alternative ("but/whereas"), followed by the noun phrase "двадцать восемь рублей" (accusative/genitive numerals for an amount) giving the corrected sum.
segments.p2-3-g215: Мне показалось is an impersonal past construction: показалось (past neuter of показаться) with a dative experiencer мне, meaning "it seemed to me." The dative first puts emphasis on the experiencer.
segments.p2-3-g216: что вдруг и так неожиданно предложить себя будет даже очень красиво is a content clause introduced by что (that). The core is an infinitival idea предложить себя (to offer oneself) functioning as the semantic subject of the impersonal predicate будет красиво ("will be beautiful/appropriate"); вдруг, и так, неожиданно are adverbial modifiers (suddenly / and so / unexpectedly) and даже очень intensifies красиво ("even very"), producing the sense "that to offer myself so suddenly and unexpectedly would even be very beautiful."
segments.p2-3-g217: и они все будут разом побеждены и посмотрят на меня с уважением is a coordinated clause: и introduces "they too" (они все) as subject; будут побеждены is a future passive (they will be defeated/overcome), разом ("at once") gives timing, and посмотрят (future, 3rd pl.) + на меня (look at me) with с уважением ("with respect") gives the resultant action and manner.
segments.p2-3-g218: Разве вы тоже хотите? is a surprised/rhetorical question: разве introduces doubt or surprise (roughly "really?"), вы is the polite/plural subject, тоже means "also/too," and хотите is present tense 2nd-person plural of хотеть (to want).
segments.p2-3-g219: - с неудовольствием заметил Симонов, is a speech tag: с + noun in instrumental (неудовольствием) expresses manner "with displeasure," заметил is past tense (here "remarked/observed"), and the surname follows the verb as the nominative subject (common in reported speech tags).
segments.p2-3-g220: как-то избегая глядеть на меня. is a participial phrase modifying Симонов's manner: как-то (colloquial adverb "somehow/for some reason") + избегая (gerund meaning "avoiding") + глядеть на меня (infinitive + preposition phrase "to look at me"); it describes simultaneous action accompanying the speaking.
segments.p2-3-g233: «А» is a linking/interjectory particle here (like 'and'/'well') connecting this question to the prior remark. «где» is the adverb 'where' (place). «вас» is the accusative/formal-object pronoun 'you'. «было» is the neuter past of 'to be' used impersonally with an infinitive to form a modal/epistemic construction ('was one to.../was it possible to...'). «искать» is the infinitive 'to look for'. Together this literally reads 'And where was it to look for you?' meaning 'Where was one supposed to look for you?'.
segments.p2-3-g234: «грубо» is an adverb 'rudely/coarsely' modifying the verb. «ввязался» is past tense, perfective, reflexive form of 'ввязаться' meaning 'to get into / to engage oneself (in a dispute or interruption)'; here it describes a sudden, forceful intervention. The name «Ферфичкин» comes after the verb—common Russian word order for vivid narration—so it is the subject ('Ferfichkin') who performed the action.
segments.p2-3-g235: «Вы» is the polite/formal (or plural) second-person pronoun 'you' as subject. «всегда» is the adverb 'always'. «были» is the past-tense plural (or polite) of 'to be' and here marks a past/characteristic state. The phrase «не в ладах» is an idiomatic use of the noun 'лад' in plural meaning 'in harmony/terms' preceded by negation 'не' → 'not on good terms' or 'at odds'. «с Зверковым» uses the preposition + instrumental to indicate 'with Zverkov'.
segments.p2-3-g236: «прибавил» is the past tense, masculine form of 'прибавить' meaning here 'added' in the sense of adding to speech. «Трудолюбов» is the speaker's surname (subject) placed after the verb. «нахмурившись» is a gerund (деепричастие) formed from 'нахмуриться' meaning 'having frowned' or 'frowning', indicating a simultaneous manner of speaking.
segments.p2-3-g237: «Но» is the adversative conjunction 'but'. «я» is the pronoun 'I'. «уж» here is an emphatic particle meaning 'already' (often colloquial). «ухватился» is past tense, perfective, reflexive of 'ухватиться' meaning 'to grab/seize hold (of something, an opportunity, or a topic)'. «и» links the two actions. «не выпускал» is the past imperfective with negation 'did not let go' (literally 'did not release'), indicating continued action — he seized hold and kept holding.
segments.p2-3-g62: И is a simple connective ('and'/'also') linking this clause to the previous thought. The phrase не из выгоды какой-нибудь is negated: не + prepositional-type phrase with из meaning 'out of' or 'for the sake of', and выгоды ('advantage') modified by the postpositive indefinite какой-нибудь meaning 'any'. Увивались is past‑tense, plural, reflexive (увиваться) used here in the sense 'to fawn' or 'to court' — the negative scope covers the whole phrase: 'they did not fawn on him out of any advantage.'
segments.p2-3-g63: А here is contrastive ('but'), and так is an adverb meaning 'so'/'simply' or 'in that way' — together they mark the contrast to the previous negative clause: 'but simply / rather.'
segments.p2-3-g64: Из‑за того is a fixed causal expression meaning 'because of that' or 'because', built from из + за + того; грамматически the whole phrase introduces the reason clause that follows. Here того is the genitive demonstrative 'that', and the construction governs the following subordinate clause.
segments.p2-3-g65: Что introduces the content clause ('that ...'). Он is the subject 'he'; фаворизированный is a past participle/adjective meaning 'favored' (passive/participial formation) describing him; дарами природы uses the instrumental plural to mean 'by the gifts of nature' (i.e. endowed by nature); человек is the noun 'man/person' that completes the predicate: 'that he was a man favored by nature's gifts.'
segments.p2-3-g66: This whole clause is an impersonal, past habitual construction. Притом же = 'moreover/besides' (with the emphatic particle же). Как‑то is 'somehow' or 'for some reason' (как + то forming an adverb). Принято было у нас считать is an impersonal formula: принято было + infinitive means 'it was customary/accepted to ...' and у нас 'among us'. Считать Зверкова специалистом = 'to consider Zverkov a specialist', with специалистом in the instrumental. По части = 'with regard to / in the matter of' and governs ловкости и хороших манер ('dexterity and good manners').
segments.p2-3-g67: Последнее is a nominalized adjective meaning 'the latter/the last [thing mentioned]' and is the subject; меня is the accusative object 'me'; особенно is an adverb 'especially/most of all'; бесило is past neuter singular of бесить and agrees with the neuter subject Последнее — here it means 'infuriated/annoyed (me)'.
segments.p2-3-g68: Я ненавидел is straightforward past tense 'I hated'. Резкий is an adjective modifying звук (which follows) and here carries the meaning 'sharp/harsh' describing the quality of the tone.
segments.p2-3-g69: Несомневающийся is a present active participle (acting as an adjective) meaning 'not doubting' or 'self‑assured' and modifies звук. В себе is a reflexive phrase 'in himself' (i.e. 'self‑confident'). Звук is the head noun 'sound/tone', and его голоса is the genitive attribute 'of his voice' specifying whose tone it is.
segments.p2-3-g70: Обожание is a noun 'adoration' and is another object of ненавидел. Собственных своих is a double possessive (собственных + своих) used for emphasis 'his own' / 'one's very own'. Острот is the genitive plural of острота here meaning 'witticisms' or 'sharp remarks.'
segments.p2-3-g71: Которые is the relative pronoun referring back to 'острот' (witticisms). У него выходили is a Russian phrasing meaning 'they came out from him / when he produced them' (у indicates possession/source and выходили is the past plural of выходить used in the sense 'turned out'). Ужасно is an adverb intensifier ('terribly/very'), and глупы is the short‑form plural adjective 'stupid', agreeing with 'остроты'.
segments.p2-3-g72: Хотя is 'although'. Он был is 'he was'. И here functions as an intensifying/adding particle ('even/also'), and смел на язык is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'bold of tongue' or 'bold in speech' (смел = brave/bold; на + язык indicates boldness in speech).
segments.p2-3-g73: Again Я ненавидел 'I hated' begins the second clause. Его красивое is a nominalized adjective phrase 'his handsome [one]' where красивое anticipates the noun лицо that follows; the phrase functions as the direct object of ненавидел.
segments.p2-3-g74: Но introduces contrast ('but'); глупенькое is a diminutive/adversative adjective meaning 'a little silly' or 'rather silly'; лицо is the noun 'face'. На которое begins a relative clause ('on/for which') pointing to that face; я бы uses the conditional particle бы with the pronoun я indicating the unreal conditional ('I would ...').
segments.p2-3-g75: Впрочем is a parenthetical adverb here meaning 'however' or 'indeed' and softens or qualifies the following remark (it introduces the speaker's concession).
segments.p2-3-g76: Променял is the past tense of променять 'to exchange/ trade away' used in the conditional from the earlier 'я бы променял' ('I would have traded'). С охотою is an archaic adverbial phrase 'with eagerness / willingly' (охотою = willingly). Свое умное is 'my clever [one]' (the speaker's own clever face), where умное is a nominalized adjective. И links to the final object: развязно‑офицерские приемы сороковых годов — a compound adjective 'licentiously/officerly' modifying приемы ('manners/ways'), with сороковых годов in the genitive plural 'of the forties (years)'.
segments.p2-3-g194: "Из деликатности" is a prepositional phrase meaning "out of delicacy/courtesy," where из + genitive (деликатности) gives the motive. "примет" is the 3rd person singular future (perfective) of принять, here meaning "he will accept" or "he'll take (it)." The clause is an adverbial motive + main verb and prepares the contrast with the next clause.
segments.p2-3-g195: The pair "но зато" forms a contrasting connective meaning "but/on the other hand." "от себя" literally "from himself," here idiomatically "from his own (pocket)" (source). "полдюжины" is the quantity "half a dozen" as the object, and "выставит" is future 3rd person singular of выставить (perfective) meaning "will put up/contribute/present." The verb at the end (SOV order) gives the clause its finalizing emphasis.
segments.p2-3-g196: «Ну» is an interjection used to open the utterance—here functioning as English "well" (a prompt/softener) before the question.
segments.p2-3-g197: «куда» here is interrogative meaning "to what end/what good" (not a literal location). «нам» is dative "to us," and «четверым» is the collective numeral in dative meaning "the four (of us)." «полдюжины» names the quantity "half a dozen" (the thing whose usefulness is questioned). The sequence asks rhetorically "what use is a half-dozen to the four of us?"
segments.p2-3-g198: «заметил» is the past tense, masculine, singular of заметить and here means "remarked" (a speech tag). «Трудолюбов» is the speaker's surname (nominative).
segments.p2-3-g199: «обратив» is the adverbial participle (деепричастие) from обратить meaning "having turned" or "directing." It governs the action of the matrix verb (заметил): "remarked, having turned his attention..." «внимание» is the noun "attention" (accusative after обратить), «только» restricts it to "only/merely," and «на полдюжину» (на + accusative) indicates the object of attention: "to the half-dozen."
segments.p2-3-g200: «Так» here is a discourse marker meaning "so/therefore/all right," used to signal a summary or decision before the enumerated details.
segments.p2-3-g201: «трое» is the collective numeral meaning "three (people)" and is used here as a brief count ("three"). It stands alone as a summarized headcount.
segments.p2-3-g202: «с Зверковым» uses the preposition с + instrumental (Зверковым) to mean "with Zverkov," and «четверо» is the collective numeral "four (people)." The phrase together states that including Zverkov there are four.
segments.p2-3-g203: «двадцать один рубль» is the amount "twenty-one rubles" (numeral + noun). «в Hotel de Paris» is a locative/time/appointment phrase using в to indicate the place (at the Hotel de Paris) where the payment/meeting will occur.
segments.p2-3-g204: «завтра» is the adverb "tomorrow." «в пять часов» is the standard time expression "at five o'clock," with в marking the time and часов (plural genitive) following the numeral.
segments.p2-3-g205: «окончательно» is an adverb meaning "finally" or "definitively." «заключил» is past tense masculine singular of заключить meaning "(he) concluded" (i.e., finished the arrangement), and «Симонов» is the subject's surname (nominative).
segments.p2-3-g206: «которого» is the relative pronoun in accusative masculine singular referring back to Симонов and functioning as the object "whom." «выбрали» is past plural of выбрать meaning "they chose/elected," and «распорядителем» is the instrumental noun meaning "as the organizer/steward" (indicating the role he was chosen for).
segments.p2-3-g207: Как here introduces a question equivalent to English 'how/about what' (an elliptical 'what about.../how about...'), and the particle же adds emphasis or insistence (colloquial/contrastive). The numeral двадцать один stands alone as the topic of the question (nominative).
segments.p2-3-g221: Он is the nominative personal pronoun 'he' (subject). Знал is the past tense, masculine singular form of знать, agreeing with он; here it means 'knew' (simple past). Меня is the accusative object 'me', and наизусть is an adverb meaning 'by heart' placed after the verb to qualify how he knew me.
segments.p2-3-g222: This is an impersonal emotional construction: взбесило is the past neuter form of взбесить meaning 'drove (someone) mad' or 'infuriated'; the experiencer is expressed by the fronted accusative меня ('me'). The verb's neuter form makes the clause feel like 'it [the fact] drove me mad.'
segments.p2-3-g223: Что introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). Он знает is present indicative ('he knows' / 'he is acquainted with'), with знает in the 3rd person singular present. Меня is the accusative object and наизусть is the adverb 'by heart', forming 'that he knows me by heart.'
segments.p2-3-g224: Почему is the interrogative 'why'. Же is an emphatic particle adding force or insistence ('then/after all'). The final -с is a colloquial/enclitic particle found in older speech (here written attached to the question), not a preposition; it softens or colors the question and has no direct literal translation.
segments.p2-3-g225: Я is the nominative subject 'I'. Ведь is a particle meaning 'after all' or 'you know', giving a justificatory or emphatic tone to the clause and attaching to the subject for emphasis.
segments.p2-3-g226: Кажется is a parenthetical present-form of казаться meaning 'it seems' or 'I seem', used here to hedge the assertion; it functions like 'it seems' / 'apparently' at the start of the clause.
segments.p2-3-g227: Тоже is an adverb 'also / too'. Товарищ is a predicate noun in the nominative ('fellow' / 'comrade'); Russian often omits the copula, so 'Я... тоже товарищ' = 'I am a fellow too.'
segments.p2-3-g228: И is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this clause to the following parenthetical confession.
segments.p2-3-g229: Признаюсь is first-person singular present of признавать(ся) meaning 'I admit' or 'I confess'; here it is a parenthetical verb introducing the speaker's frank remark.
segments.p2-3-g230: Мне is the dative experiencer 'to me' and обидно is a short-form predicate meaning 'it is offensive / I am offended'; together with даже ('even') this yields 'I'm even offended' or 'it even offends me'. Note the change from accusative (меня) earlier to dative (мне) for expressing emotional effect.
segments.p2-3-g231: Что introduces the subordinate clause 'that they passed me by.' Меня is the accusative 'me', and обошли is past plural of обойти: here it means 'they passed (me) by / overlooked me / passed me over', with the subject omitted (indefinite 'they').
segments.p2-3-g232: Заклокотал is the past masculine form meaning 'burst into a gurgling/chattering sound' or 'began to chatter'. Было here is the particle-like use of было indicating an attempted or momentary action ('had begun to...'), placed after the verb in colloquial style; я is the subject 'I'; опять is 'again'. The sequence gives a narrated aside: '— I began chattering again.'
segments.p2-3-g159: Он был из тех + noun in genitive is an idiom meaning “he was one of those …”. Был is the past tense of быть; почитателей is genitive plural (object of из), and Зверкова is the genitive of the surname Zverkov, showing possession/association (admirers of Zverkov).
segments.p2-3-g160: This is a relative clause introduced by которые (“who/which”) referring back to почитателей. Заигрывали is imperfective past plural meaning “flirted/played at flirting”; с ним uses the preposition с + instrumental to mean “with him/toward him.” Из видов is an idiom here meaning “out of appearances/for the sake of appearances” (из + genitive вид/видов). Занимали у него деньги: занимали (past, plural) here means “borrowed,” у него marks the lender/source (“from him”), and деньги is the direct object (“money”).
segments.p2-3-g161: A simple noun phrase: Другой гость (another/other guest) with Симонова in the genitive to show possession (‘Simonov’s other guest’). The adjective/noun sequence is nominative.
segments.p2-3-g162: A single-word apposition: the surname Трудолюбов in nominative, set off by commas and referring to the same person.
segments.p2-3-g163: Была личность незамечательная is a predicative clause: была (past feminine of быть) agrees with личность (feminine) and незамечательная is the predicate adjective meaning “unremarkable.” Note Russian sometimes uses 'была личность' where the verb agrees with the noun личность rather than the preceding name.
segments.p2-3-g164: Военный парень is an attributive adjective + noun: военный here means “military” (adjective) modifying парень (“young man”/“fellow”). It describes his profession/role.
segments.p2-3-g165: Высокого роста is a fixed genitive phrase literally “of tall height” used to mean “tall.” Высокого is an adjective in genitive singular modifying роста (genitive of рост).
segments.p2-3-g166: С + instrumental (холодною физиономией) describes appearance: холодною is an archaic/informal instrumental form of холодный, and физиономией is instrumental of физиономия ‘physiognomy/face’ — “with a cold face.”
segments.p2-3-g167: Довольно (adverb) + честный (adjective) = “fairly/honestly” — an adverbial modifier qualifying his character.
segments.p2-3-g168: This participial/infinitival cluster qualifies him: но (but) introduces contrast. Преклонявшийся is a past participle meaning “one who bowed/revered” and governs the following prepositional phrase перед + instrumental (перед всяким успехом = “before/at the sight of any success”). Способный + infinitive (рассуждать) = “capable of reasoning/discussing”; только об одном производстве restricts the topic to “only about one kind of production.”
segments.p2-3-g169: Зверкову is dative (to/for Zverkov) and frames the relation: он доводился кем-то is a construction meaning “he turned out to be / proved to be someone to (Zverkov).” Кaким-то is the indefinite (“some/so-and-so”) and takes the particle -то; дальним родственником is in the instrumental as the role/relationship (to be someone’s relative).
segments.p2-3-g170: И это — a short clause linking the previous fact with its consequence: и = and, это = this/that. The demonstrative pronoun refers to the relation just mentioned.
segments.p2-3-g171: Глупо сказать is a parenthetical phrase meaning “foolish (or stupid) to say” / “foolish as it may be to say,” acting adverbially to soften the following claim. Сказать is the infinitive; глупо is an adverb.
segments.p2-3-g172: Придавало is past neuter singular (agreeing with это) of придавать “to give/impart/attribute” and here means “gave” or “lent.” Ему is the indirect object “to him.” Между нами (“between/among us”) localizes the social circle; некоторое значение is the direct object “some/small importance” (некоторое = a certain, значение = significance/standing).
segments.p2-3-g173: Меня (accusative) fronted for emphasis: Меня он считал — “He considered me …”. Считал is past masculine singular of считать “to consider/think.” Ни во что is an idiomatic negative phrase meaning “as nothing/for nothing” (ни intensifies the negation; во + что conveys the idea ‘into nothing’).
segments.p2-3-g174: Обращался (past, masculine) here means “addressed/treated” (imperfective). Же is an emphatic particle; хоть introduces a concessive clause (“though/even if”) — хоть не совсем вежливо = “though not entirely politely.” Хоть + не совсем + adverb is a common concessive pattern.
segments.p2-3-g175: Но сносно is a brief concluding remark: но = but/yet, and сносно is an adverb meaning “tolerably/acceptably.” It concedes that his manner, while not fully polite, was bearable.
segments.p2-3-g176: 'Что ж' is a common conversational particle combination meaning 'well/so' or 'well then'; 'Что' here functions as an exclamatory/introductory particle and 'ж' is a reinforcing particle that makes the phrase more colloquial and conclusive.
segments.p2-3-g177: 'коль по семи рублей' is a conditional phrase: 'коль' = 'if/seeing that'; 'по' + numeral expresses a per-unit price ('по семи рублей' = 'at seven rubles apiece'); 'рублей' is the genitive plural of 'рубль' required after 'по' with numerals.
segments.p2-3-g178: 'заговорил Трудолюбов' is the narrator's tag: 'заговорил' is the perfective verb meaning 'began to speak/said' used to introduce direct speech, and 'Трудолюбов' is the speaker's surname in nominative.
segments.p2-3-g179: 'нас трое' is an impersonal/collective ordinal expression meaning 'there are three of us' (literally 'us, three'); 'нас' behaves like a genitive/partitive with this numeric existential construction rather than a simple direct object.
segments.p2-3-g180: 'двадцать один рупь' gives the total sum: 'двадцать один' = 'twenty-one', and 'рупь' is an archaic variant/spelling of 'рубль' (ruble) used here in the accusative/nominal sense to state the amount.
segments.p2-3-g181: 'можно хорошо пообедать' is an impersonal modal construction: 'можно' = 'one can/it is possible', followed by the adverb 'хорошо' modifying the infinitive 'пообедать' ('to have lunch' / 'to dine'), yielding 'we could have a very good lunch.'
segments.p2-3-g182: Here 'Зверков' is the proper name of a person; although the lemma list gives animal senses, in the text this is the man's surname used in nominative as the subject of the sentence.
segments.p2-3-g183: 'конечно' is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'of course' or 'naturally', placed between subject and predicate to comment on the statement's obviousness.
segments.p2-3-g184: 'не платит' is a present-tense indicative: 'не' negates the verb 'платит' ('pays'), so the clause states a simple present fact/habit—'doesn't pay' or 'won't pay' in conversational English.
segments.p2-3-g185: 'Уж разумеется' is an emphatic phrase: 'уж' strengthens the following adverb and 'разумеется' (literally 'it is understood') functions as 'of course'—a fixed parenthetical expression expressing certainty.
segments.p2-3-g186: 'коль мы же его приглашаем' is a subordinate conditional/explanatory clause: 'коль' = 'since/if', 'мы' = 'we', 'же' is an emphatic particle ('we ourselves' / stressing the subject), 'его' is the accusative 'him', and 'приглашаем' is present-tense 'invite'—'since we are inviting him.'
segments.p2-3-g187: 'решил Симонов' is the reporting clause: 'решил' (past tense) here means 'decided', and 'Симонов' is the nominative surname of the speaker who makes that decision.
segments.p2-3-g188: 'Неужели ж вы думаете' is a rhetorical question opener: 'Неужели' expresses incredulity ('do you really/surely'), 'ж' adds emphasis, 'вы' is the addressee 'you', and 'думаете' is present-tense 'think'—together: 'Do you really think...?'
segments.p2-3-g189: 'заносчиво и с пылкостию ввязался Ферфичкин' is the narrator's interruption tag: 'заносчиво' (adverb) = 'arrogantly', 'и' = 'and', 'с' = 'with', 'пылкостию' is an instrumental noun meaning 'zeal/ardor' (here in an older/dialectal form), 'ввязался' is perfective past 'butted/entered (into the discussion)' and 'Ферфичкин' is the speaker's name.
segments.p2-3-g190: 'точно нахал лакей' is a simile: 'точно' = 'as if'/'exactly like', 'нахал' = 'an impudent person', and 'лакей' = 'lackey/footman'—so 'as if an impudent lackey', with the order matching Russian noun phrases 'adverb + noun + noun'.
segments.p2-3-g191: 'хвастающий звездами своего генерала барина' is a participial modifier describing that lackey: 'хвастающий' is a present participle 'boasting', 'звездами' is instrumental plural ('with/using stars'—here referring to the stars/insignia), 'своего' = 'his/one's own', 'генерала' = 'general' (office), and 'барина' = 'barin' ('master/sir') used ironically—so literally 'boasting with the stars of his general/master.'
segments.p2-3-g192: The repeated 'неужели вы думаете' restates the incredulous question: same structure as before—'do you really think...?'—used for rhetorical emphasis before the subordinate clause.
segments.p2-3-g193: 'что Зверков нас пустит одних платить?' is the subordinate clause introduced by 'что' = 'that' (here introducing the content of 'think'): 'Зверков' (proper name) is subject, 'нас' = 'us' (accusative), 'пустит' is future/present perfective 'will let/allow', 'одних' (accusative plural) = 'alone/the only ones', and 'платить' = infinitive 'to pay'—'that Zverkov will let us be the only ones to pay?'.
segments.p2-3-g291: Гм is an interjection (an onomatopoeic hum) expressing hesitation, thought, or a noncommittal response. The form is invariable and here stands alone as a brief utterance.
segments.p2-3-g294: Да here is the particle used as a simple affirmative reply meaning “yes.” As a one-word answer it carries confirmation or agreement; punctuation and prosody determine force (here neutral/short).
segments.p2-3-g297: Так functions as a connective/adverb meaning “then” or “in that case,” drawing a conclusion or decision; завтра is the adverb “tomorrow.” This is an elliptical, colloquial scheduling phrase (the verb is omitted: e.g. “(We’ll do it) then, tomorrow”). Word order (так + завтра) emphasizes the resulting time.
segments.p2-3-g298: Деньги is the nominative plural noun 'money' (topic). The enclitic -то attached to Деньги is a contrastive/emphatic particle that highlights or presses the noun (‘the money’ specifically). Вы is the subject pronoun (formal singular or plural), placed after the topicalized noun for emphasis; отдадите is the future tense, 2nd person plural form of the perfective verb отдать (‘to give back/return’), forming a single-word future; теперь is the temporal adverb ‘now’ placed at the end to mark time. The whole is a direct question asking for confirmation about returning the money, with word order and -то stressing the object.
segments.p2-3-g266: Довольно here is an interjection meaning 'enough' or 'that's enough', used to stop or cut off further talk; punctuation marks the end of the quoted utterance.
segments.p2-3-g267: сказал is the past tense, masculine singular of сказать ('to say'), used in the speech-attribution formula; Трудолюбов is the speaker's surname in the nominative, following the verb as the named subject.
segments.p2-3-g268: вставая is a деепричастие (gerund/adverbial participle) meaning 'rising/getting up', indicating an attendant/ simultaneous action to the main verb (he spoke while getting up).
segments.p2-3-g269: This is a conditional clause: Если ('if') introduces the condition; ему is a dative experiencer ('to him' — 'he felt'); так уж очень is a stack of intensifiers ('so/indeed very'); захотелось is an impersonal perfective past form of захотеться meaning 'felt like' or 'wanted', with the experiencer in dative.
segments.p2-3-g270: пусть is a jussive/imperative particle meaning 'let (him)', and придет is the 3rd-person singular (perfective) form of прийти ('to come'); together they form a permissive command: 'let him come'.
segments.p2-3-g271: Да ведь is a colloquial exclamatory particle combination adding emphasis ('but/after all'); у нас uses the preposition у + pronoun to express possession ('we have'); кружок свой literally 'circle (club) our own' — кружок is 'circle/club' and свой is the reflexive possessive 'one's own', referring back to the group.
segments.p2-3-g272: приятельский is an adjective 'of friends' / 'friendly' modifying кружок and characterizing the circle as informal and made up of friends; placed as an appositive adjective after a comma for emphasis.
segments.p2-3-g273: злился is the past imperfective reflexive of злиться ('to be angry / to take offense'), masculine singular, reporting his angry tone; Ферфичкин is the speaker's name in the nominative.
segments.p2-3-g274: тоже 'also' links this action to others (he too does it); берясь is a деепричастие of браться here meaning 'taking hold' or 'seizing' rather than 'beginning'; за + accusative (за шляпу) indicates the object grasped ('at/by the hat'); шляпу is the accusative of шляпа ('hat').
segments.p2-3-g275: Это is a demonstrative pronoun/subject ('this/it'); не negates the predicate; официальное is an adjective 'official' modifying собрание; собрание is a neuter noun in the nominative meaning 'meeting/gathering' — the clause states that the gathering is not formal.
segments.p2-3-g238: "Мне кажется" is an impersonal introductory phrase: the dative pronoun 'Мне' + the present-tense verb 'кажется' (казаться) mean 'it seems to me'. There is no explicit grammatical subject; the construction frames the following clause as the speaker's impression.
segments.p2-3-g239: Об этом is a prepositional phrase (об + это in the prepositional case) meaning 'about this/that'. Никто (no one) is the subject and не вправе судить is the predicate: 'не вправе' is a predicative adjective meaning 'is not entitled' or 'has no right', followed by the infinitive судить 'to judge'. The word order is standard: topical prepositional phrase, subject, negated predicative + infinitive.
segments.p2-3-g240: Возразил is a past‑tense, masculine singular verb (I objected/retorted) and is placed before the pronoun я for stylistic emphasis. The phrase с дрожью в голосе uses с + instrumental (дрожью) and the prepositional phrase в голосе to mean 'with a tremor in (my) voice' — an adverbial modifier describing how he spoke.
segments.p2-3-g241: Точно here functions as a colloquial particle 'as if' (not literal 'exactly'), introducing the following parenthetical clause. И бог знает что случилось is a loose, idiomatic remark: и (an intensifying/linking particle), бог знает (present tense, 'God knows'), followed by a subordinate clause что случилось ('what happened'); together it conveys 'as if — God only knows what happened.'
segments.p2-3-g242: Именно потому-то is a cluster where именно ('exactly') and потому ('for that reason') are given extra emphasis by the particle то attached to потому (потому-то) — together meaning 'that's precisely why'. The following pronoun я is topicalized and set off by a comma.
segments.p2-3-g243: Можно read 'может быть' as a parenthetical modal expression meaning 'perhaps/maybe'; grammatically it's the modal verb может (here expressing possibility) plus infinitive быть, functioning together to soften the assertion.
segments.p2-3-g244: Теперь 'now' locates the action in time; и here is an emphatic particle ('even/also') that strengthens либо the idea (теперь и хочу = 'now I even want to'), and хочу is the present first‑person singular 'I want' serving as the main predicate of this clause.
segments.p2-3-g245: Что introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason: 'that/because'. Прежде был не в ладах is a past-tense clause: прежде ('before/previously'), был (past of быть 'was'), не в ладах literally 'not in ladi' — an idiom meaning 'not in harmony' or 'out of sorts/at odds'. The negation 'не' applies to the predicate 'в ладах'.
segments.p2-3-g246: Ну is an interjection often translated 'well' or 'now', used here to introduce a reply with a colloquial tone.
segments.p2-3-g247: Кто вас поймет is a rhetorical question: кто ('who') plus the object вас ('you') and the verb поймет (future, 3rd person singular of понять) meaning 'will understand'. The future tense here expresses a general, indefinite 'who will be able to understand you?'
segments.p2-3-g250: Возвышенности is a plural noun meaning 'loftinesses' or 'airs' (pejoratively 'haughtiness'). The particle то after the noun is an emphatic/contrasting particle (here adding a dismissive stress), and эти is the demonstrative 'these' modifying the whole phrase; the inverted order gives a brusque, contemptuous remark: 'these very airs' / 'these loftinesses.'
segments.p2-3-g77: Я ненавидел is a past-tense, imperfective verb phrase meaning ‘I hated’ or ‘I used to hate.’ The demonstrative pronoun то is a placeholder object meaning ‘the fact/that,’ pointing forward to the subordinate clause introduced by что.
segments.p2-3-g78: что introduces the content clause (‘that’). Он рассказывал is past imperfective, indicating he kept telling/narrating (habitually or repeatedly). О своих будущих успехах с женщинами is a prepositional phrase: своих is a reflexive possessive referring back to он; будущих успе́хах is in the prepositional plural 'about future successes with women.' The parenthetical begins with a new subject он and reports his hesitation (не решался) + infinitive начинать (to begin) with women.
segments.p2-3-g79: Не имея is a gerund (деепричастие) meaning ‘not having’ or ‘without having,’ giving the circumstance for the previous clause. Ещё is ‘yet/still,’ and офицерских эполет is genitive plural ‘officer’s epaulettes.’ The whole phrase functions adverbially (parenthetical cause).
segments.p2-3-g80: и ждал их с нетерпением continues the parenthesis: ждал (imperfective past) = ‘awaited/waited for,’ их = ‘them’ (the epaulettes), and с нетерпением = ‘with impatience.’ The closing parenthesis finishes and the coordinating и introduces о том — 'and about that' (той/том used to refer to the following clause).
segments.p2-3-g81: как introduces the second subordinate clause meaning ‘how’ or ‘that … in what manner.’ Поминутно is an adverb ‘minute by minute’ (constantly). Будет выходить is the imperfective future (будет + infinitive) expressing repeated or ongoing future action: ‘will be going out’ (here: ‘challenge/appear for duels repeatedly’). На дуэли is the fixed collocation 'to duel' (на дуэль/на дуэли).
segments.p2-3-g82: Помню is present tense 'I remember' and introduces a recollection; it's a simple declarative present used to report a past scene being recalled.
segments.p2-3-g83: как я begins the content clause 'how I …' with как functioning as a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'how'/'when' and я the nominative subject 'I.'
segments.p2-3-g84: всегда молчаливый is an adjectival phrase in apposition to я: 'always silent.' It describes a habitual character trait (всегда = always; молчаливый = silent).
segments.p2-3-g85: вдруг сцепился с Зверковым: вдруг = 'suddenly'; сцепился is the past of сцепиться used here colloquially to mean 'got into a quarrel/lock horns'; с + instrumental (Зверковым) marks the opponent (with Zverkov).
segments.p2-3-g86: когда он introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when he …' with когда = 'when' and он = 'he' as subject of the following participial phrase.
segments.p2-3-g87: толкуя is a gerund (деепричастие) from толковать meaning here colloquially 'talking/speculating'; раз в свободное время = 'once/in his free time' (раз = once/occasion), с товарищами = 'with comrades,' о будущей клубничке = 'about the forthcoming klubnichka' (a diminutive/slangy notion, here a little treat/entertainment). Разыгравшись наконец is another gerund meaning 'having grown playful at last.' The simile как молодой щенок на солнце describes the manner.
segments.p2-3-g88: вдруг объявил = 'suddenly declared/announced' (объявил past tense). The repetition of вдруг emphasizes the abruptness of the announcement.
segments.p2-3-g89: что introduces reported content: ни одной ... не оставит без внимания is a negative construction with ни ... не ('not a single … will he leave without attention'); деревенской девы is genitive ('a village maiden'), в своей деревне = 'in his own village' (своей reflexively referring to the subject), and оставить (оставит) is perfective future 'will leave.'
segments.p2-3-g90: что это - droit de seigneur: the matrix clause continues with a copular construction 'that this — droit de seigneur,' where это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this' and the dash marks an appositive foreign phrase left untranslated.
segments.p2-3-g91: * а мужиков introduces a new object: а (contrastive 'and/but') and мужиков is the accusative (or colloquial plural) 'the peasants/men' — the group he threatens. The asterisk marks a footnote in the printed text.
segments.p2-3-g92: если осмелятся протестовать is a conditional clause: если = 'if'; осмелятся is the future of осмелиться (perfective) meaning 'dare/venture' (so 'if they dare to'), and протестовать is the infinitive 'to protest.'
segments.p2-3-g93: всех пересечет uses пересечёт (future, perfective) figuratively 'he will cut down/put an end to them all' (пересечь can mean to stop or cut off). И всем им ties the next action to the same people: всем им (dative plural) = 'to all of them.'
segments.p2-3-g94: бородатым канальям is dative plural describing the addressees of the previous verb (всем им): 'to the bearded scoundrels' (a pejorative epithet). The adjective and noun are in dative to agree with 'всем им.'
segments.p2-3-g95: вдвое наложит оброку: вдвое = 'twofold/twice' (adverb), наложит is future perfective 'will impose,' and оброку is the noun obrok (feudal rent) in the (here archaic) form used with наложить — meaning 'he will impose twice the rent.' The construction indicates an increase of dues.
segments.p2-3-g96: Наши хамы аплодировали: наши = 'our' (plural possessive), хамы = 'boors/rude fellows,' and аплодировали is past plural 'applauded.' The clause is a straightforward past-tense statement.
segments.p2-3-g97: я же сцепился и вовсе не из жалости к девам и их отцам: я же is contrastive 'I, on the other hand'; сцепился (past) = 'took him on/got into a quarrel'; и вовсе не из жалости = 'and not at all out of pity' (вовсе intensifies the negation не); к девам и их отцам uses к + dative to indicate toward whom the pity would be directed.
segments.p2-3-g98: а просто за то is a short transition: а = 'but/and,' просто = 'simply/merely,' and за то here means 'for the reason that' (introducing the clause of motive).
segments.p2-3-g99: что такой козявке так аплодировали: что = 'that' introducing the reason clause; такой козявке is dative (or colloquial usage) meaning 'such a little nobody/thing' (козявка = small insignificant creature), так аплодировали = 'applauded so' or 'applauded like that' (так marks the manner/degree).
segments.p2-3-g100: Я тогда одолел: Я = 'I,' тогда = 'then' (time adverb), одолел is past perfective 'overcame/defeated' — here 'I won/was the victor' in the encounter.
segments.p2-3-g101: но Зверков: но = 'but/however' introducing the contrast; Зверков is the personal name (the opponent). The clause sets up the counterpoint about him.
segments.p2-3-g102: хоть и глуп был is a concessive clause: хоть и = 'although/even though,' глуп is the short-form adjective used predicatively 'stupid/foolish,' and был is the past copula 'was.' The short-form adjective gives a vivid, concise predication.
segments.p2-3-g103: но был весел и дерзок: continuing the contrast, but (но) he was cheerful (весел — short-form adjective used predicatively) and дерзок (short-form adjective 'bold/insolent'). Both adjectives describe his temperament and explain his reaction.
segments.p2-3-g104: а потому отсмеялся и даже так: а потому = 'and therefore/so,' отсмеялся is perfective 'laughed off' (dismissed by laughter), и даже так intensifies the clause: 'and even so' or 'and laughed in such a way.'
segments.p2-3-g105: что я begins the subordinate clause describing the result: что = 'that' and я = 'I' (subject) of the following clause.
segments.p2-3-g106: по правде is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'to tell the truth' or 'in truth.' По is a preposition used in idioms here and правде is the noun 'truth' in the dative/instrumental idiomatic form.
segments.p2-3-g107: не совсем и одолел: negative construction 'not completely' (не совсем) with the past perfective одолел meaning 'I did not entirely win/defeat him' — the и is a slight emphatic/connecting particle here.
segments.p2-3-g108: смех остался на его стороне: смех (laughter) is the noun subject; остался (past) = 'remained' and на его стороне = 'on his side' is a prepositional phrase indicating which party retained the laughter — an idiom meaning the audience sided with him.
segments.p2-3-g253: усмехнулся is the past tense, masculine singular of the perfective verb усмехнуться, meaning a brief, often ironic smile; the verb appears before the subject here (inverted order), a common word-order for short stage directions after a dash. Трудолюбов is the nominative surname that names the subject who performed the action.
segments.p2-3-g254: Вас is the 2nd-person formal/object pronoun (accusative), the direct object of запишут. Запишут is 3rd-person plural future tense of записать (perfective) and here functions impersonally/actively: 'they will write down/register you' (i.e. 'you will be registered').
segments.p2-3-g255: решил is the past tense, masculine singular of решить ('decided'). It serves as a reporting verb for the speech that precedes and follows, indicating Simonov's internal decision as he speaks.
segments.p2-3-g256: обращаясь is a verbal adverb (деепричастие) meaning 'addressing' or 'while addressing'; it expresses an accompanying action. ко мне is the preposition (ко, variant of к used before m-sounds) + dative pronoun meaning 'to me' and specifies the addressee of Simonov's words.
segments.p2-3-g257: Симонов is the nominative surname of the speaker and functions as the subject for решил; its placement after the participial phrase and reporting verb is typical in Russian narrative for identifying the speaker.
segments.p2-3-g258: завтра is an adverb meaning 'tomorrow' giving the day. в ... часов is a standard time expression where в (here used for time) + numeral + часов (genitive) yields 'at five o'clock'; часов is the genitive plural of час used after numerals.
segments.p2-3-g259: в with a location proper name indicates place ('at/in'). Нotel (here a proper name, Hotel de Paris) names the establishment where the registering will occur; the foreign name is treated as a locative destination after в.
segments.p2-3-g260: не is the negative particle 'not.' ошибитесь is the 2nd-person plural (or polite singular) form of the perfective verb ошибиться; though morphologically future indicative ('you will be mistaken'), such a form with не functions as a polite or emphatic negative imperative: 'don't make a mistake / don't be mistaken.'
segments.p2-3-g261: Деньги is the plural nominative noun 'money' (here used exclamatorily). The enclitic particle -то attached to the noun is an emphatic/contrastive particle (often colloquial) that draws attention or marks contrast—so the phrase conveys an abrupt reminder or objection: 'but the money!'
segments.p2-3-g262: начал is the past tense, masculine singular of начать ('began'); было here is a parenthetical particle (the past form of быть used after a verb) that often indicates the action was attempted or about to occur but then interrupted. Ферфичкин is the nominative subject; вполголоса is an adverb meaning 'in a low voice' or 'half-whisper,' modifying how he began.
segments.p2-3-g263: кивая is a verbal adverb (деепричастие) from кивать meaning 'nodding.' на меня is the preposition на + accusative pronoun 'me' meaning 'at me/toward me'; Симонову is the dative form of the name Симонов and indicates the person to whom the nod was directed—i.e. he nodded to Simonov, indicating me. Together they mean 'nodding to Simonov toward me' or 'nodding to Simonov to indicate me.'
segments.p2-3-g264: но is the coordinating conjunction 'but.' осекся is the past tense, reflexive verb meaning 'cut himself off' or 'fell silent'—it describes that he stopped speaking abruptly.
segments.p2-3-g265: потому что is the causal conjunction 'because.' даже is an adverb meaning 'even' (emphasizing the unexpectedness), Симонов is the nominative name (subject of the clause), and сконфузился is the past tense, perfective reflexive verb meaning 'became embarrassed' or 'was embarrassed.' The whole clause gives the reason for the interruption: 'because even Simonov became embarrassed.'
segments.p2-3-g321: Хорошо here is an adverbal interjection used as a brief spoken response meaning 'all right' or 'okay'. The initial dash marks reported speech; the word stands alone as a reply rather than modifying another verb.
segments.p2-3-g322: A repeat of хорошо for emphasis; grammatically identical to the previous segment, functioning as a parenthetical/emphatic interjection (not comparative). The surface form is the adverb (though the provided lemma is the adjective хороший).
segments.p2-3-g323: Все равно is an idiomatic phrase: все ('everything/everyone') + равно ('equal') together mean 'it's all the same' or 'it doesn't matter.' Here both words form a fixed predicate meaning 'it makes no difference.'
segments.p2-3-g324: Расплатитесь is the 2nd-person plural form of the perfective verb расплатиться; in this context it functions as a polite/plural imperative or a directive 'pay/settle up' addressed to one or more people. Завтра is an adverb of time 'tomorrow.' За + обедом (instrumental) indicates the time during which or at which the settling will occur — 'at/over lunch.'
segments.p2-3-g325: Я is the subject 'I.' ведь is a discourse particle ('after all' / 'you know') giving an explanatory or justificatory tone. только means 'only/just' and limits what follows; the comma marks a pause before the purpose clause.
segments.p2-3-g326: чтоб (colloquial variant of чтобы) introduces a purpose clause 'in order that / so as to,' followed by the infinitive знать 'to know.' The clause is elliptical: English would supply a verb ('I asked' or 'I came') but Russian leaves it implied, so the sense is 'I only [did it] in order to know.'
segments.p2-3-g329: Вы is the nominative second-person pronoun used here as a direct address; it can be plural or the polite singular (formal you). The comma marks a vocative-style pause when addressing someone.
segments.p2-3-g330: пожалуйста is an interjection meaning 'please' used to make a polite request or invitation; here it invites the addressee to act (e.g. 'go ahead').
segments.p2-3-g135: Года в три is an idiomatic temporal phrase meaning ‘at/about thirty (years old)’ (literally “years in three”); it sets the age. Он очень опустился uses the past reflexive verb опуститься (опустился) meaning ‘fell/declined/abase himself’ or ‘went downhill’ socially or morally; очень simply intensifies it.
segments.p2-3-g136: Хотя introduces a concessive clause ‘although’. Был is past tense ‘was’; по-прежнему is a frozen adverbial (по + прежнему) meaning ‘as before / still’. Довольно modifies the short-form predicate adjectives красив и ловок (‘handsome and nimble’); both adjectives are in short form and predicate position.
segments.p2-3-g137: Как-то here is the particle/compound как-то (‘somewhat / somehow / one day’) modifying отек; taken with отек (past of отёк) it says he ‘grew swollen/puffy’ — a physical change. The two pieces как + то form the adverbial particle rather than a comparative.
segments.p2-3-g138: Стать in past (стал) + infinitive жиреть produces the periphrastic meaning ‘began to …’: ‘he began to get fat.’ Стал is present/starting sense here, not stative.
segments.p2-3-g139: Видно было is an impersonal construction: видно = ‘apparently/visible’, with было (past neuter of быть) giving ‘it was apparent’. It introduces the speaker’s observation about what followed.
segments.p2-3-g140: Что introduces the subordinate clause ‘that…’. К тридцати годам is a dative time phrase with к meaning ‘by (the time of)’ → ‘by the age of thirty’. Он совершенно обрюзгнет is future simple (обрюзгнуть, 3rd sg) meaning ‘he will become coarse/flabby’; совершенно intensifies it.
segments.p2-3-g141: Вот is an emphatic particle (‘this/now/see’). Этому-то is the dative demonstrative with the emphatic particle -то, meaning ‘to this very one’ (the -то stresses exactness). Уезжавшему is a past active participle in the dative (‘to the one who was leaving’), and Зверкову is the dative of the name — the indirect object. The verb phrase и хотели дать places the intention (хотели) and infinitive дать ‘to give’ near the end; наши товарищи is the subject placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis.
segments.p2-3-g142: Они (they) is the subject; постоянно (constantly) and все три года (throughout all three years) are adverbial/time expressions. Водились с ним is past plural of водиться with the sense ‘to associate with / keep company with’ (verb used reflexively/impersonally for recurring socializing).
segments.p2-3-g143: Хотя begins a concessive clause ‘although’; сами is emphatic ‘themselves’ (they themselves, not others).
segments.p2-3-g144: Внутренно is an adverb meaning ‘inwardly / internally’ and qualifies the following thought (their real feelings).
segments.p2-3-g145: Не считали is past plural with negation ‘did not consider’; себя is the reflexive direct object ‘themselves’. С ним (‘with him’) here marks comparison/association, and на равной ноге is an idiom ‘on equal footing’ (на + adjective in feminine + ноге). The whole phrase = ‘did not consider themselves on equal terms with him.’
segments.p2-3-g146: Я уверен в этом is a straightforward comment: subject ‘I’ + predicative adjective уверен ‘sure’ with the prepositional object в этом ‘of that/about this’ — the narrator’s assurance.
segments.p2-3-g147: Из двух гостей Симонова is a genitive phrase ‘of Simonov’s two guests’ (из + genitive). Один был Ферфичкин is a simple past identification: ‘one (of them) was Ferfichkin’ with был as past copula.
segments.p2-3-g148: Из русских немцев is an appositive/genitive group meaning ‘one of the Russian Germans’ (Germans in Russia); из + genitive marks membership/part of a group.
segments.p2-3-g149: Маленький ростом uses ростом (instrumental) to mean ‘in stature/height’ — a common Russian construction: ‘small in stature.’
